title: 10 best herbal tinctures for colds
intro_desc: Herbal tinctures for colds are concentrated liquid extracts of medicinal herbs, created by soaking plant material in a mixture of water and alcohol. These tinctures capture the beneficial compounds of the herbs, making them easily accessible for absorption by the body. Some popular herbal tinctures used to alleviate cold symptoms include echinacea, elderberry, and goldenseal. Echinacea is known to boost the immune system, reducing the severity and duration of colds. Elderberry has antiviral properties that can help lessen symptoms and shorten the cold's duration. Goldenseal has antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects, providing relief for sore throats and congestion. By incorporating these herbal tinctures into their wellness routines, individuals can experience reduced cold symptoms and faster recovery times, ultimately improving their overall quality of life during cold season.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Yarrow tincture helps with colds because it contains anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and astringent properties that work together to alleviate symptoms. The herb's anti-inflammatory effects can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe. Its antiseptic properties can help fight off the virus causing the cold, while its astringent properties can help dry up a runny nose and soothe a sore throat. Additionally, yarrow has been shown to stimulate the immune system, helping the body fight off the cold more effectively. Overall, yarrow tincture can provide relief from cold symptoms and help the body recover more quickly.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Yarrow tincture's anti-inflammatory properties, due to compounds like flavonoids and sesquiterpene lactones, help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating cold symptoms.

Antipyretic: Yarrow's ability to reduce fever (antipyretic property) is crucial during colds, as it helps regulate body temperature and combat infection, thanks to active constituents like alkaloids and volatile oils.
Diaphoretic: Yarrow encourages perspiration (diaphoretic property), promoting the elimination of toxins and providing relief from cold symptoms, attributed mainly to lactones and flavonoids.

Antimicrobial: Yarrow tincture's antimicrobial properties inhibit the growth of cold-causing bacteria and viruses, contributing to faster recovery, due to the presence of volatile oils and flavonoids.

Immune-boosting: Yarrow supports the immune system (immune-boosting property) during cold infections, enhancing the body's ability to fight off pathogens, owing to active constituents such as alkaloids, flavonoids, and polysaccharides.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 handfuls of fresh yarrow flowers and leaves, ensuring they are fully grown and free from pesticides.
Chop the yarrow into small pieces and fill a clean glass jar, leaving 1-inch of space at the top.
Pour 80-proof vodka or rum over the chopped yarrow until it is completely submerged, sealing the jar tightly.
Store the jar in a cool, dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking it gently once a day.

Strain the liquid using cheesecloth or a fine mesh strainer into a dark glass bottle, discarding the spent plant material. The resulting tincture can be stored in a cool, dark place for up to a year. Take 1-2 mL up to three times a day as needed for cold symptoms.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Yarrow flowers are rich in azulene, a compound known for its anti-inflammatory and fever-reducing properties, making them an ideal addition to cold-relieving tinctures.
Leaves: The leaves of the yarrow plant contain high amounts of flavonoids and sesquiterpene lactones, which have been shown to help alleviate symptoms of colds such as congestion and cough.
Stems: Yarrow stems contain essential oils that have been found to have antimicrobial and antiviral properties, providing additional benefits when used in medicinal tinctures for colds.
Seeds: The seeds of the yarrow plant are a rich source of fatty acids, which can help soothe inflamed mucous membranes and support the immune system during cold episodes.
Roots: Yarrow roots are known for their astringent, diaphoretic, and diuretic properties, making them a valuable component in tinctures aimed at treating cold symptoms and reducing fever.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tincture helps with colds because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes slippery when it comes into contact with water. This mucilage coats and soothes the throat, reducing irritation and inflammation. Additionally, marshmallow tincture has expectorant properties, which help to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. This can help to alleviate symptoms such as coughing and congestion. Furthermore, marshmallow tincture has anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties, which can help to reduce the severity and duration of a cold. Overall, marshmallow tincture can provide relief from cold symptoms and support the body's natural defenses.
remedy_properties: Demulcent: Marshmallow tincture creates a protective film on mucous membranes, soothing irritation and reducing coughs, making it easier to manage cold symptoms. The active constituents responsible for this property are polysaccharides like mucilage.

Anti-inflammatory: Marshmallow tincture reduces inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating discomfort and congestion associated with colds. The flavonoids and phenolic acids in marshmallow are responsible for its anti-inflammatory effects.

Expectorant: Marshmallow tincture helps loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and expel phlegm. The high mucilage content in marshmallow contributes to its expectorant properties.

Antitussive: Marshmallow tincture can help suppress coughing by soothing the throat and reducing irritation. The demulcent and anti-inflammatory properties of marshmallow contribute to its antitussive effects.

Immunomodulatory: Marshmallow tincture may help regulate the immune response, potentially reducing the severity and duration of cold symptoms. The polysaccharides in marshmallow have been shown to have immunomodulatory effects.
remedy_recipe: Chop 1 cup of dried herbs, such as elderberry, echinacea, and licorice root, into small pieces.
Combine herbs and 2 cups of 80 proof vodka in a quart-sized jar, ensuring herbs are fully submerged.
Seal the jar and store in a cool, dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking daily.
Strain the mixture using cheesecloth, squeezing out excess liquid.
Store tincture in a glass bottle with a tight-fitting lid, away from direct sunlight.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties, marshmallow leaves help soothe sore throats and reduce symptoms of colds.
Flowers: Marshmallow flowers contain mucilage, which helps relieve irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating cold symptoms.
Roots: High in mucilage and antitussive properties, marshmallow roots are effective in suppressing coughs and soothing irritated mucous membranes during colds.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wormwood tincture helps with colds because it has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that can alleviate symptoms and potentially shorten the duration of a cold. Wormwood, also known as Artemisia absinthium, contains compounds such as thujone and flavonoids that have been shown to have a positive effect on the immune system, helping the body to fight off viruses and infections. Additionally, wormwood tincture can help to reduce fever, relieve cough and congestion, and soothe a sore throat. Its warming and stimulating properties can also help to boost energy levels and improve overall well-being during a cold.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial: Wormwood tincture has antimicrobial properties that can help eliminate the underlying infection causing a cold, due to the presence of compounds like thujone, isothujone, and camphor.

Anti-inflammatory: Its anti-inflammatory properties help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms like coughing and sore throat, thanks to compounds like beta-myrcene and limonene.
Expectorant: Wormwood tincture can help loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe, due to the presence of sesquiterpene lactones such as artabsin and absinthin.
Immune-boosting: Wormwood tincture can stimulate the immune system, helping the body fight off the cold virus, due to the presence of antioxidants and polyphenols.
Analgesic: Its analgesic properties help alleviate pain and discomfort associated with colds, including headaches and muscle aches, thanks to compounds like thujone and chamazulene.
remedy_recipe: Chop 2 ounces of dried wormwood herb into small pieces and place in a clean glass jar.
Pour 1 pint of vodka or grain alcohol (80-100 proof) over the herbs until they are completely covered.
Seal the jar tightly and store in a cool, dark place for 2-6 weeks, shaking daily.
Strain the tincture using cheesecloth into a clean, dark glass bottle and discard the herbs.
Label the bottle with the name of the tincture, ingredients, and date, and store in a cool, dark place. Recommended dosage is 1-2 mL up to three times a day.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Wormwood leaves are often used in medicinal tinctures for colds due to their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties.
Flowers: The flowers of wormwood contain volatile oils and flavonoids, making them useful in tinctures to help alleviate cold symptoms.
Seeds: Wormwood seeds are rich in thujone, a compound known for its antispasmodic and expectorant properties, which can be beneficial in treating colds.
Stems: Although less potent than the leaves and flowers, wormwood stems can still contribute to tinctures, providing additional plant compounds that may help with cold relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eastern purple coneflower tincture helps with colds because it contains powerful anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. This herbal remedy, also known as Echinacea, has been used for centuries to boost the immune system and fight off infections. It works by stimulating the production of white blood cells, which help to defend the body against viruses and bacteria. Additionally, eastern purple coneflower tincture has been shown to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms such as sore throat, cough, and fever. By taking this tincture at the first sign of a cold, you can help to shorten the duration of the illness and reduce the severity of symptoms.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Eastern purple coneflower tincture can help fight off the bacteria and viruses that cause colds, due to the presence of active constituents such as diterpenoids and sesquiterpene lactones.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The tincture can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms such as coughing and sore throat, thanks to the anti-inflammatory agents like cichoric acid and flavonoids.

Immune-boosting properties: Eastern purple coneflower tincture can strengthen the immune system, helping the body fight off colds more effectively, due to the immune-enhancing compounds like polysaccharides and essential oils.

Antioxidant properties: The tincture can help protect cells from damage caused by free radicals, which can exacerbate cold symptoms, due to the high antioxidant content, including phenolic acids and vitamins.

Diaphoretic properties: Eastern purple coneflower tincture can promote sweating, which can help the body eliminate toxins and reduce fever, providing relief from cold symptoms, due to the diaphoretic action of compounds like volatile oils and alkaloids.
remedy_recipe: Harvest purple coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) flowers and leaves at peak bloom, chop into small pieces, and fill a clean jar halfway.
Add 80-proof vodka until the jar is full, ensuring all plant material is submerged, then secure a lid on the jar.
Store the jar in a cool, dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking it gently every few days.
After the maceration period, strain the mixture using cheesecloth or a fine mesh sieve, squeezing out the liquid and discarding the plant material.
Store the resulting tincture in a glass dropper bottle and use 20-30 drops up to 3 times a day for cold prevention and treatment.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in medicinal compounds, the flowers of eastern purple coneflower are used to make tinctures for treating colds due to their anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties.
Leaves: High in antioxidants, the leaves of the eastern purple coneflower are used in medicinal tinctures for colds, as they help alleviate symptoms and support overall immune function.
Roots: Packed with healing properties, the roots of the eastern purple coneflower are used to make potent tinctures for treating colds, as they have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects.
Seeds: Containing essential oils and beneficial compounds, the seeds of the eastern purple coneflower are used in medicinal tinctures for colds, as they offer antispasmodic and expectorant benefits.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Siberian ginseng tincture helps with colds because it contains active compounds that can boost the immune system and increase the body's resistance to infections. It has been shown to stimulate the production of immune cells, such as natural killer cells and T-lymphocytes, which help to fight off viruses and bacteria that cause colds. Additionally, Siberian ginseng has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with colds, such as sore throat and congestion. By strengthening the immune system and reducing inflammation, Siberian ginseng tincture can help to prevent the onset of colds and shorten their duration.
remedy_properties: Adaptogenic property: Siberian ginseng tincture helps the body adapt to physical, chemical, and biological stressors, which is important for colds because it can enhance the immune system's response to viral infections. The active constituents responsible for this property are eleutherosides, polysaccharides, and phenylpropanoids.

Immunostimulant property: Siberian ginseng tincture stimulates the immune system, which is crucial for colds because it can reduce the severity and duration of the infection. The primary active constituents responsible for this property are eleutherosides and polysaccharides.

Antioxidant property: Siberian ginseng tincture has antioxidant properties that help protect cells from damage caused by free radicals, which is significant for colds because it can support the body's overall health and resistance to infections. The active constituents responsible for this property are phenolic acids, flavonoids, and eleutherosides.

Anti-inflammatory property: Siberian ginseng tincture has anti-inflammatory effects, which is beneficial for colds because it can alleviate symptoms such as fever, sore throat, and inflammation in the respiratory tract. The active constituents responsible for this property are eleutherosides, saponins, and phenylpropanoids.

Antiviral property: Siberian ginseng tincture has antiviral properties that can inhibit the replication of viruses, which is vital for colds because it can prevent the spread and worsening of the infection. The active constituents responsible for this property are eleutherosides, polysaccharides, and phenylpropanoids.
remedy_recipe: Chop 1 ounce of dried Siberian ginseng root into small pieces and place in a clean glass jar.
Pour 2 cups of 100-proof vodka or pure grain alcohol over the herbs, ensuring they are fully submerged.
Secure a lid on the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for 2-4 weeks, shaking it gently every few days.
Strain the mixture using a cheesecloth or fine mesh strainer, discarding the solids and reserving the liquid.
Store the tincture in a glass dropper bottle and use 1-2 dropperfuls up to 3 times daily at the onset of a cold.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Siberian ginseng are commonly used to make medicinal tinctures for colds due to their adaptogenic properties, which help to boost the immune system and increase resistance to stress.
Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes of Siberian ginseng contain active compounds that have antiviral and anti-inflammatory effects, making them effective in treating colds and other respiratory infections.
Leaves: The leaves of Siberian ginseng are rich in antioxidants and have been shown to have immunostimulatory effects, making them useful for preventing and treating colds.
Seeds: The seeds of Siberian ginseng contain high levels of eleutherosides, which have been shown to have antipyretic and analgesic effects, making them useful for treating symptoms of colds.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Horsetail tincture helps with colds because it contains an abundance of antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that can strengthen the immune system and alleviate cold symptoms. Horsetail is known for its high silica content, which can support the health of the respiratory system and promote the healing of damaged tissues. Additionally, horsetail tincture can help to reduce inflammation in the throat and airways, making it easier to breathe and reducing the discomfort associated with a cold. The herb's natural diuretic properties can also help to eliminate toxins from the body, further supporting the immune system and promoting overall health and well-being.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial: Horsetail tincture has antimicrobial properties that can help reduce the severity of a cold by inhibiting the growth of bacteria and viruses responsible for the infection. This is due to the presence of phenolic acids and flavonoids.

Anti-inflammatory: Horsetail tincture has anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of a cold, such as a sore throat, cough, and congestion, by reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract. The active constituents responsible for this property include saponins and tannins.

Antioxidant: Horsetail tincture has antioxidant properties that can help strengthen the immune system and reduce oxidative stress caused by a cold. This is due to the presence of flavonoids, phenolic acids, and other polyphenols.
Diuretic: Horsetail tincture has diuretic properties that can help reduce fluid retention and congestion caused by a cold. This is due to the presence of equisetin and other triterpenes.

Expectorant: Horsetail tincture has expectorant properties that can help clear mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and reducing the severity of a cold. The active constituents responsible for this property include saponins and flavonoids.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 ounces of fresh horsetail herb, ensuring it is fully grown and free of pollutants; rinse and dry.
Chop the horsetail into small pieces and place in a clean, dry jar, filling it three-quarters full.
Pour 10 ounces of 80-proof vodka or food-grade alcohol over the horsetail, ensuring it is fully submerged.
Seal the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for a minimum of two weeks, shaking it daily.
Strain the mixture using cheesecloth, squeezing out the excess liquid; store the resulting tincture in a dark glass bottle with a tight-fitting lid.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: Horsetail rhizomes contain silica and other minerals that can help boost the immune system and soothe sore throats.
Stems: Horsetail stems are rich in antioxidants that can help reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms of colds.
Leaves: Horsetail leaves contain flavonoids and phenolic acids that have antiviral and antibacterial properties, making them useful for treating colds.
Flowers: Although not commonly used, horsetail flowers can be used in tinctures and contain compounds that can help alleviate congestion and coughs.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice tincture helps with colds because it has antiviral and expectorant properties that can reduce phlegm and soothe a sore throat. It also has anti-inflammatory effects that can help alleviate symptoms such as coughing and congestion. Additionally, licorice tincture can boost the immune system, making it easier for the body to fight off infections. The herb contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that has been shown to inhibit the replication of viruses, making it a useful tool in preventing and treating colds. Furthermore, licorice tincture can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from symptoms such as chest congestion and difficulty breathing.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Licorice tincture loosens and thins mucus, making it easier to cough up and clear respiratory passages, which is crucial for treating cold-related congestion. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhizin.

Demulcent: Licorice tincture forms a soothing film over mucous membranes, providing relief from irritation and inflammation, which is beneficial for soothing sore throats caused by colds. The active constituents responsible for this property are glycyrrhizin and flavonoids.
Anti-inflammatory: Licorice tincture reduces inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms associated with colds. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhizin.
Antiviral: Licorice tincture inhibits the replication of viruses, including those causing the common cold. The active constituents responsible for this property are glycyrrhizin and flavonoids.
Antitussive: Licorice tincture suppresses coughing, providing relief from continuous coughing fits caused by colds. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhizin.
remedy_recipe: Chop 1 cup of dried licorice root into small pieces and place in a clean glass jar.
Pour 2 cups of 80-proof vodka or grain alcohol over the licorice root, ensuring it is fully submerged.
Seal the jar tightly and store in a cool, dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking the jar gently every few days.
After 4-6 weeks, strain the mixture through cheesecloth into a clean glass bottle, squeezing out any excess liquid.
Store the finished licorice tincture in a cool, dark place and use 1-2 mL up to three times daily for cold relief.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, which has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, making it useful for treating colds.
Seeds: Licorice seeds are used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory ailments, including colds, due to their expectorant and soothing properties.
Bark: The bark of the licorice plant contains compounds that have been shown to have antiviral and anti-inflammatory effects, making it a potential remedy for colds.
Leaves: Licorice leaves are used in herbal medicine for their demulcent properties, which can help soothe a sore throat and ease coughing associated with colds.
Flowers: Licorice flowers have been used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory ailments, including colds, due to their expectorant and soothing properties.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English ivy tincture helps with colds because it contains potent antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties. The herb's active compounds, including saponins and flavonoids, work together to alleviate symptoms and reduce the duration of a cold. English ivy tincture helps to soothe a sore throat, relieve congestion, and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Its expectorant properties make it an effective remedy for coughs and chest infections. Additionally, it strengthens the immune system, enabling the body to fight off the cold virus more efficiently. Overall, English ivy tincture is a valuable natural remedy that can provide relief and support during cold season.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: English ivy tincture helps loosen and remove mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel phlegm and alleviating congestion. This property is important for colds as excess mucus can lead to breathing difficulties and prolonged illness. The active constituents responsible for this effect are saponins, which have been shown to have expectorant properties.

Antispasmodic: English ivy tincture can help relax smooth muscles, reducing coughing and spasms in the respiratory tract. This is crucial for colds as coughing can exacerbate symptoms and lead to further irritation. The primary active constituents responsible for this effect are flavonoids and saponins.

Antimicrobial: English ivy tincture has antimicrobial properties that can help fight off the viruses and bacteria responsible for causing colds. This is important for colds as it can help shorten the duration of the illness and reduce the severity of symptoms. The active constituents responsible for this effect are saponins and polyacetylenes.

Anti-inflammatory: English ivy tincture has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms such as sore throat and irritation. This is important for colds as inflammation can exacerbate symptoms and lead to discomfort. The active constituents responsible for this effect are flavonoids and saponins.

Immune-boosting: English ivy tincture can help strengthen the immune system, making it more effective at fighting off infections. This is important for colds as a strong immune system can help prevent the onset of illness or reduce the severity of symptoms. The active constituents responsible for this effect are flavonoids and polyphenols.
remedy_recipe: Harvest English ivy leaves, ensuring they are fully grown and free from pesticides, then rinse them thoroughly.
Chop the leaves finely and fill a clean glass jar, leaving a 1-inch space at the top, before adding 80-proof vodka until the leaves are fully submerged.
Seal the jar tightly and store it in a cool, dark place for two to six weeks, shaking it daily to ensure proper extraction.
Strain the tincture using cheesecloth or a fine mesh strainer, discarding the leaves and storing the liquid in a clean, dark glass bottle.
Label the bottle with the preparation date, ingredient, and dosage, recommending 20-40 drops up to three times a day for cold relief.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the English ivy plant contain polyphenols and flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and expectorant properties, making them useful for treating colds.
Stems: The stems of the English ivy plant contain saponins, which have expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties, making them helpful for relieving symptoms of colds.

Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the English ivy plant contain high levels of saponins and polyphenols, which have antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, making them useful for treating colds and respiratory infections.
Bark: The bark of the English ivy plant contains tannins and saponins, which have astringent and expectorant properties, making it useful for treating colds and respiratory infections.

Buds: The buds of the English ivy plant contain high levels of polyphenols and flavonoids, which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, making them helpful for treating colds and respiratory infections.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St. John's Wort tincture helps with colds because it has antiviral and antibacterial properties, which can help the body fight off the viruses and bacteria that cause colds. Additionally, it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe. The herb also contains compounds that can help boost the immune system, which can help the body better defend itself against infection. Furthermore, St. John's Wort is known to have a mild sedative effect that can help alleviate some of the symptoms associated with colds, such as a sore throat and cough. Overall, St. John's Wort tincture can provide relief from cold symptoms while also helping to shorten the duration of the illness.
remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: St. John's wort tincture can help reduce the severity of colds by inhibiting the replication of viruses, thanks to the presence of hypericin and hyperforin.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The tincture can alleviate cold symptoms such as sore throat and stuffy nose by reducing inflammation, due to the action of hypericin and flavonoids.
Immune system boosting: St. John's wort tincture can enhance the body's defense against colds by stimulating the immune system, attributed to the presence of hypericin and hyperforin.
Antidepressant properties: The tincture can help manage cold-related stress and anxiety by regulating neurotransmitter levels, owing to the action of hypericin and hyperforin.
Antioxidant properties: St. John's wort tincture can protect cells from damage caused by cold viruses by scavenging free radicals, due to the presence of flavonoids and hypericin.
remedy_recipe: Harvest St. John's Wort flowers and leaves, ensuring they are fully dried and free from moisture.
Fill a clean jar halfway with the dried herb, then add 80-proof vodka until the jar is completely full.
Seal the jar tightly and store it in a cool, dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking it gently once a day.
After the recommended time, strain the mixture using cheesecloth or a fine mesh strainer, discarding the solid plant material.
Store the resulting St. John's Wort tincture in a dark glass bottle with a tight lid, keeping it in a cool, dark place for up to a year. Recommended dosage for colds is 30 drops, 3 times a day.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in hypericin and hyperforin, St. John's Wort flowers are used to make tinctures that help alleviate cold symptoms by reducing inflammation and fighting off viruses.
Leaves: High in antioxidants, St. John's Wort leaves are used in tinctures to support the immune system during cold episodes, while providing soothing relief for irritated respiratory tracts.
Buds: Packed with active compounds, St. John's Wort buds create potent tinctures that help combat cold-related fatigue and promote overall well-being during cold season.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English lavender tincture helps with colds because it has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation and fight off infection. The herb contains compounds called tannins and flavonoids that have been shown to help relieve symptoms such as sore throats, coughs, and congestion. Additionally, lavender has calming and relaxing effects, which can help alleviate stress and promote better sleep, both of which are important for aiding in a quick recovery. English lavender tincture can be taken orally, providing a convenient and natural way to help manage cold symptoms.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: English lavender tincture can help inhibit the growth of bacteria and viruses that cause colds, thanks to its active constituents like linalool, lavandulol, and eucalyptol.

Antispasmodic properties: The soothing effect of lavender tincture on the respiratory tract helps alleviate coughing and reduces muscle spasms, making it easier to manage cold symptoms, due to compounds like linalyl acetate and linalool.

Analgesic properties: English lavender tincture can help relieve pain and discomfort associated with colds, such as headaches and sore throats, as a result of its active constituents, including linalool, eucalyptol, and camphor.

Anxiolytic properties: Lavender tincture may help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation during cold symptoms, allowing the body to rest and recover, due to its active constituents like linalool and lavandulol.
Decongestant properties: English lavender tincture can help clear nasal passages and alleviate congestion, making it easier to breathe, by means of active constituents like eucalyptol and camphor.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh English lavender flowers, filling a clean, dry mason jar halfway.
Add 80-proof vodka until the flowers are completely submerged, covering by at least 1 inch.
Securely seal the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking it gently once a day.
After the maceration period, strain the mixture using cheesecloth, squeezing out the liquid and discarding the spent flowers.
Store the finished lavender tincture in a tightly sealed glass bottle, away from light and heat, for up to a year.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in essential oils, the flowers of English lavender are used in tinctures for their calming and soothing properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of colds.
Leaves: High in antioxidants, lavender leaves are used in tinctures to support the immune system and reduce inflammation, providing relief during a cold.
Buds: Packed with therapeutic compounds, lavender buds are used in tinctures for their potent antimicrobial and antispasmodic properties, helping to fight off infections and ease coughing.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat colds with herbal tinctures is by using a combination of echinacea, goldenseal, and elderberry. Echinacea boosts the immune system, while goldenseal has antimicrobial properties. Elderberry, on the other hand, has antiviral effects and helps reduce inflammation. Take the tincture every few hours at the onset of cold symptoms. However, it's important to note that while herbal tinctures can alleviate symptoms and support recovery, they are not a cure for the common cold. Always consult with a healthcare provider before starting any new supplement regimen.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to colds that are treated with herbal tinctures include sore throats, coughs, and congestion. Echinacea is a popular tincture used to boost the immune system and reduce inflammation, often taken at the first sign of a cold. Elderberry tincture is used to reduce mucus production, alleviate sinus pressure, and soothe a sore throat. Licorice root tincture can soothe a cough and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract. Ginger tincture can help alleviate symptoms of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea that can sometimes accompany a cold. As with any medication, it is important to consult with a healthcare provider before starting any new herbal remedies.
